questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force exerted by a proton on another proton is
questions.solution:
A proton has mass, so it exerts a gravitational force on another proton.

A proton is a charged particle, so it exerts an electromagnetic force on another proton.

A proton is a nucleon, so it exerts a strong nuclear force on another proton.

A proton is composed of fermions (quarks), so it also interacts via the weak nuclear force.

Therefore, a proton exerts all four fundamental forces on another proton.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
According to Newton's third law of motion, to every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

Action and reaction forces always act on two different bodies. Therefore, they act on two distinct objects.

These forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

Since they act on different bodies, they cannot be added together to yield a zero resultant force on a single body.

Thus, action and reaction forces act on two distinct objects, point in opposite directions, and possess equal magnitudes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force exerted by a neutron on a proton is
questions.solution:
Both the neutron and the proton have mass, so they exert a gravitational force on each other.

They are both nucleons, so they interact via the strong nuclear force.

Both particles are composed of quarks and participate in weak interactions, so they exert a weak force on each other.

Although the neutron has zero net electric charge, it is composed of charged quarks and possesses a non-zero magnetic dipole moment. Therefore, it exerts an electromagnetic force on the proton.

Thus, all four types of forces are exerted by a neutron on a proton.

questions.subject: Physics
options: The electromagnetic force between a pair of protons may be greater than the nuclear force between them.
The gravitational force between a pair of protons may be greater than the nuclear force between them.
The electromagnetic force between a pair of protons is always greater than the gravitational force between them.
The nuclear force between a pair of protons is always greater than the electromagnetic force between them.
questions.solution:
The nuclear force is a short-range force, effective only over distances of the order of a few femtometers ($10^{-15}$ m). Beyond this range, it decreases exponentially and becomes negligible.

The electromagnetic and gravitational forces are long-range forces, both following the inverse-square law ($1/r^2$).

At large distances, the nuclear force is practically zero, while both electromagnetic and gravitational forces are non-zero. Therefore, at such distances, both the electromagnetic force and the gravitational force between a pair of protons are greater than the nuclear force between them. This makes statements (1) and (2) correct, and statement (4) incorrect.

The ratio of the electromagnetic force to the gravitational force between two protons is given by $\dfrac{F_e}{F_g} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\dfrac{e^2}{r^2}}{G\dfrac{m_p^2}{r^2}} = \dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 G m_p^2}$.

This ratio is a constant independent of the separation distance $r$ and is approximately $10^{36}$. Thus, the electromagnetic force is always greater than the gravitational force between a pair of protons. This makes statement (3) correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Both ends of a spring are displaced along its length. Every displacement has an identical magnitude. In which of the following cases does the tension or compression within the spring attain a maximum magnitude?
options: The right end is shifted leftward and the left end rightward.
The right end is shifted rightward and the left end leftward.
questions.solution:
Let the magnitude of displacement of each end be $x$.

The tension or compression in a spring is proportional to the change in its length.

When the right end is shifted leftward and the left end rightward, the spring is compressed by $2x$. The magnitude of deformation is $2x$.

When both ends are shifted in the same direction (both leftward or both rightward), the net change in the length of the spring is zero.

When the right end is shifted rightward and the left end leftward, the spring is elongated by $2x$. The magnitude of deformation is $2x$.

Since the maximum change in length is $2x$, the tension or compression attains a maximum magnitude in the first and fourth cases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which among the following systems can be sufficiently described by classical physics?
options: The motion of a cricket ball
The motion of a dust particle
A neutron transforming into a proton
questions.solution:
Classical physics is sufficient to describe the motion of macroscopic objects moving at non-relativistic speeds.

A cricket ball and a dust particle are macroscopic objects, so their motion can be described by classical mechanics.

A hydrogen atom and a neutron transforming into a proton are microscopic and subatomic systems, respectively. These require quantum mechanics for an accurate description.

Therefore, the motion of a cricket ball and a dust particle can be sufficiently described by classical physics.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If all matter were composed of electrically neutral particles like neutrons,
options: sitting on a chair would be impossible
the earth would be unable to move around the sun
there would be no tension in a string
the force of friction would be absent
questions.solution:
The normal force, tension in a string, and the force of friction are all macroscopic manifestations of the electromagnetic force between atoms and molecules. If all matter were composed of electrically neutral particles, electromagnetic forces would cease to exist. Consequently, normal force, tension, and friction would be absent.

The gravitational force depends only on mass and is independent of electric charge. Since neutral particles like neutrons possess mass, the gravitational force would still exist. Thus, the earth would continue to move around the sun.
